Reason (R): Inclusive green growth combines economic growth (poverty reduction, employment) with environmental sustainability, ensuring growth benefits reach the poor while preserving natural capital for future generations.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Inclusive green growth (World Bank framework) integrates: growth (raises GDP and employment), green (decouples growth from environmental damage), and inclusive (distributes benefits equitably, especially to poor and rural). For India — with massive poverty, rapid growth aspirations, and severe environmental pressures — this framework uniquely addresses all EC14 themes simultaneously. R provides the comprehensive rationale for A.

questions.question: Which of the following is the MOST direct way through which 'animal husbandry' contributes to rural development?
options: It increases urban manufacturing output
It provides year-round employment and supplementary income to small and marginal farmers, reducing seasonal unemployment vulnerability
It is exclusively associated with large commercial farms
It reduces agricultural land availability
questions.explanation: Animal husbandry (dairy, poultry, goat rearing) provides crucial income diversification for small farmers — income is earned year-round unlike seasonal crop income. It employs women significantly (dairy cooperatives), reduces seasonal unemployment, and provides nutrition (milk, eggs, meat). The Amul cooperative model demonstrates animal husbandry's transformative rural development potential.

questions.question: A solar energy project with a lifespan of 25 years costs ₹500 crore upfront. Annual benefits: ₹40 crore in electricity cost savings + ₹10 crore in carbon credits. What is the simple (undiscounted) 25-year BCR?
questions.explanation: Annual benefit = ₹40 + ₹10 = ₹50 crore. Total 25-year benefit = 50 × 25 = ₹1,250 crore. BCR = 1,250/500 = 2.5. A BCR of 2.5 > 1 justifies the investment. The carbon credit component illustrates how environmental goods (emission reductions) can be monetised to improve project economics — linking environment and economic sustainability.

questions.question: India targets becoming a $5 trillion economy. Critics argue this is meaningless without addressing the 'four deficits': skill deficit, infrastructure deficit, institutional deficit, and environmental deficit. Which combination of EC14 themes do the four deficits most directly correspond to?
options: Only GDP growth matters; deficits are irrelevant
Skill deficit → human capital; Infrastructure deficit → rural development; Institutional deficit → employment policy; Environmental deficit → sustainable development — all four EC14 pillars
All four deficits relate only to urban industrial policy
The four deficits are only relevant for developed economies
questions.explanation: Perfect one-to-one correspondence: skill deficit = inadequate human capital (education, vocational training); infrastructure deficit = barriers to rural development (roads, power, water, digital); institutional deficit = weak employment policy (labour laws, social protection, job creation); environmental deficit = unsustainable resource use threatening long-term growth. The $5 trillion target requires addressing all four — the integrated EC14 agenda.

questions.question: A graph shows a positive relationship between 'Health Expenditure per capita' and 'Labour Productivity' in rural areas, with the relationship steeper for women than men. Which economic and social explanation BEST accounts for the gender difference?
options: Women are biologically more responsive to healthcare

Women in rural India face greater baseline health deprivation; thus, marginal healthcare improves their productivity more from a lower base. Improved health also enables female labour force participation that was previously prevented by illness
Men's productivity is not affected by health
The difference is a statistical anomaly with no economic meaning
questions.explanation: Women in rural India start from a lower baseline of health access (gender discrimination in healthcare, nutrition, and autonomy). Marginal improvements in health from a lower base yield higher marginal productivity gains — diminishing returns imply greater impact where deprivation is highest. Additionally, female LFP is often constrained by health — improved health enables participation that was previously impossible, unlocking larger productivity gains.

questions.question: What is the economic justification for government regulation of groundwater extraction in India?
options: Groundwater is a private good with well-defined property rights that markets can efficiently allocate

Groundwater is a common pool resource — individual extraction imposes costs on other users and future generations that the extractor does not pay; government regulation corrects this market failure to prevent tragedy of the commons
Groundwater regulation is primarily a political decision with no economic rationale
Only urban groundwater needs regulation; rural groundwater is always sufficient
questions.explanation: Groundwater is a common pool resource: one user’s extraction reduces availability for others, while exclusion is difficult. Unregulated extraction creates a negative externality and can lead to tragedy of the commons. Government regulation through caps, permits, pricing, or recharge rules helps internalise this market failure. Option B is correct.

questions.question: India's per capita health expenditure: government = ₹1,944/year; private out-of-pocket (OOP) = ₹2,336/year (2020-21). A BPL household earning ₹60,000/year spends ₹8,000 OOP on health. What % of income is OOP, and if government health spending doubles (eliminating half the OOP burden), what is the freed income for human capital investment?
options: OOP: 13.3% of income; freed income: ₹4,000
OOP: 13.3% of income; freed income: ₹8,000
OOP: 3.3% of income; freed income: ₹2,000
OOP: 6.7% of income; freed income: ₹4,000
questions.explanation: OOP as % of income = (8,000/60,000) × 100 = 13.3%. This is catastrophic health expenditure (defined as >10% of income). If government spending doubles and halves OOP burden: freed income = ₹8,000 × 50% = ₹4,000. This ₹4,000 can now be invested in children's education or nutrition — illustrating the human capital case for universal health coverage.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): India's renewable energy target of 500 GW by 2030 serves both environmental and employment goals.
Reason (R): Renewable energy expansion creates jobs in manufacturing (solar panels, wind turbines), installation, maintenance, and rural electrification — simultaneously reducing carbon emissions and generating employment.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: India's National Solar Mission targets 500 GW renewable capacity by 2030. This doubles as climate action (displacing ~1 billion tonnes of CO₂ annually) and job creation (estimated 3.4 million jobs in renewables by 2030). R correctly identifies the employment channels — manufacturing, installation, maintenance, rural electrification — that make renewables a 'double dividend' for EC14's environment and employment pillars.

questions.question: A district collector reviews a 10-year plan: Year 1-3: Invest in rural roads and electricity. Year 4-6: Establish rural schools, health centres, skill training. Year 7-10: Support agro-processing FPOs and microfinance. Evaluate this sequencing from an EC14 integrated development perspective.
options: The sequencing is wrong — microfinance should come first

The sequencing is optimal — infrastructure (roads, electricity) first creates conditions for human capital investment (schools, health), which then enables productive enterprise development (FPOs, microfinance) — infrastructure → human capital → livelihood sequencing
All four interventions should happen simultaneously — sequencing is inefficient
The plan ignores environmental sustainability — it will fail
questions.explanation: The sequencing reflects development theory: (1) Physical infrastructure (roads, electricity) is a prerequisite for all subsequent development — schools and clinics need power and access; (2) Human capital investment (education, health, skills) builds the workforce capacity needed for enterprises; (3) Enterprise support (FPOs, microfinance) leverages the educated, healthy workforce enabled by prior investments. This virtuous sequence is the theoretical basis for India's integrated rural development approach.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST captures the 'capability approach' critique of GDP as a development measure?
options: GDP accurately measures all dimensions of human well-being

GDP measures economic output but fails to capture capabilities — what people can actually do and be (health, education, agency, dignity) — which are intrinsically valuable beyond their contribution to income
The capability approach endorses GDP as the best development metric
GDP should be replaced by income per household as the only valid development measure
questions.explanation: Amartya Sen's capability approach (foundational to HDI) argues that the ultimate goal of development is expanding human capabilities — the real freedoms people have to live lives they value. GDP captures only monetary income, missing health, education, freedom, and agency. A person may have high income but limited capabilities (e.g., chronic illness) or low income but rich capabilities (access to public goods). This critique underpins all EC14 human development indicators.

questions.question: Country X has 40 crore workers. Agricultural productivity = ₹80,000/worker/year; non-agricultural productivity = ₹3,20,000/worker/year. 50% of workers are in agriculture. If structural transformation shifts 10% of agricultural workers to non-agriculture over 10 years, what is the gain in total GDP (assuming productivity unchanged)?
options: Rs.96,000 crore
Rs.4,80,000 crore
Rs.48,000 crore
Rs.1,92,000 crore
questions.explanation: Agricultural workers = 50% of 40 crore = 20 crore. Workers shifted = 10% of 20 crore = 2 crore. Productivity gain per shifted worker = Rs.3,20,000 - Rs.80,000 = Rs.2,40,000. Total GDP gain = 2 crore workers x Rs.2,40,000 = Rs.4,80,000 crore. Option B is correct.

questions.question: An integrated assessment of India's development challenge in 2026: GDP grows at 7%; 40 crore still in agriculture (low productivity); 9 crore NEET youth (Not in Education, Employment, or Training); air and water quality degrading; rural-urban gap persisting. A NITI Aayog committee recommends a 'quadruple nexus' strategy simultaneously targeting all four EC14 pillars. Which combination of policies MOST comprehensively embodies this quadruple nexus?
options: Only infrastructure investment — all other outcomes will follow
Only GDP growth focus — trickle-down will address other dimensions

Expanding vocational education (human capital) + MGNREGS 2.0 with green works (employment + environment) + agri-diversification with FPOs (rural development) + renewable energy transition (environment + employment) — a genuinely integrated quadruple nexus
Attracting foreign investment in IT sector — solves all problems through growth
questions.explanation: The quadruple nexus requires simultaneous policy action across all four EC14 pillars: (1) Vocational education → human capital for NEET youth; (2) Green MGNREGS (e.g., watershed works, afforestation) → employment + environment simultaneously; (3) Agri-diversification with FPOs → rural development through income diversification; (4) Renewable energy → environmental sustainability + green employment. No single-pillar solution addresses India's compound development challenges. Option C uniquely integrates all four.
